content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.

A **peak** is a subarray (a contiguous subsequence) of AAA whose elements first strictly increase and then strictly decrease in value.

More formally, a subarray from LLL to RRR is a **peak** if and only if there exists an integer XXX such that L≤X≤RL \le X \le RL≤X≤R for which the subarray from LLL to XXX is strictly increasing and the subarray from XXX to RRR is strictly decreasing. Note that subarrays that are only strictly increasing and subarrays that are only strictly decreasing are considered **peaks** as well.

Find the maximum length of a **peak** in the sequence AAA.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the maximum length of a **peak** in the sequence AAA.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the maximum length of a peak in the given sequence.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the longest subarray that first strictly increases then strictly decreases (or only one direction) is reported. The lengths are 5, 3, 4 and 3 respectively.

solution.approach:
For every position i compute two auxiliary arrays: inc[i] = length of the longest strictly increasing subarray that ends at i (including i). It is 1 if i==0 or A[i] ≤ A[i‑1], otherwise inc[i]=inc[i‑1]+1. dec[i] = length of the longest strictly decreasing subarray that starts at i (including i). It is 1 if i==N‑1 or A[i] ≤ A[i+1], otherwise dec[i]=dec[i+1]+1. A peak that has its highest point at i has length inc[i] + dec[i] – 1 (the peak element is counted twice). The answer for the test case is the maximum of inc[i] + dec[i] – 1 over all i. This also covers pure increasing (dec[i]=1) and pure decreasing (inc[i]=1) subarrays. The algorithm runs in O(N) time and O(N) additional memory per test case.

Pseudo‑code:
for each test case:
    read N and array A
    inc[0]=1
    for i=1..N-1:
        if A[i] > A[i-1]: inc[i]=inc[i-1]+1 else inc[i]=1
    dec[N-1]=1
    for i=N-2 down to 0:
        if A[i] > A[i+1]: dec[i]=dec[i+1]+1 else dec[i]=1
    ans=0
    for i=0..N-1:
        ans=max(ans, inc[i]+dec[i]-1)
    print ans
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(∑N) overall

code.Python3:
import sys
def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data: return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        A = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        inc = [1]*N
        for i in range(1,N):
            inc[i] = inc[i-1]+1 if A[i]>A[i-1] else 1
        dec = [1]*N
        for i in range(N-2,-1,-1):
            dec[i] = dec[i+1]+1 if A[i]>A[i+1] else 1
        ans = 0
        for i in range(N):
            cur = inc[i]+dec[i]-1
            if cur>ans: ans=cur
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
